blocks.content: Nadia heard them from her room.
Not the words — just the voices. Cade's low, urgent. Wren's quiet and even. The sound of a conversation being had in a hallway two doors from where she was sitting on her bed in the dark.
Then Wren's footsteps going back down the stairs. Then nothing.
Nadia sat still for a long time.

She'd always known Wren was the variable. From the beginning, from the first day of the trip, she had factored Wren in — the quiet one, the one who watched instead of talking, the one whose eyes moved across a room and registered things before other people knew there was anything to register.
She noticed. She doesn't know what she noticed yet. But she's close.
Nadia looked at the door of her room. Then at the window. Then at her hands.
Eight years. Eight years and you are not going to let it fall apart now because of five minutes at a dinner table.
She stood up. Smoothed her shirt. Went downstairs to sit with the others.
Control was a discipline. You practiced it the way you practiced anything.
She had been practicing for eight years.
